region.bioregion: Northeastern Pennsylvania · Pocono Plateau · Delaware River Highlands

hero.question: What is the forest ready for this week?
hero.lead: Not a guess from a photo — a reading of moisture, trees, slope, and season. Learn what the Poconos are offering; go outside to see if the forest agrees.

regionalStatus.headline: Pike County · late spring
regionalStatus.summary: Morels fading below 1,200 feet. Chanterelles still weeks away — watch oak ridges after sustained June rain. Morning field windows best before afternoon storms.

weather.label: This week
weather.conditions: Humid; valley fog at dawn; thunderstorm chance Saturday after 3 p.m.
weather.soilMoisture: Elevated in ravines; ridge tops drying

fruitingOutlook.note: River bottoms & old orchards below 1,200 ft
Burn edges & disturbed ground — verify ID carefully
Too early — target late June on oak slopes
On dead oak after warm rain — cut small sample only

thisWeekOutdoors.title: Read moisture before you read mushrooms
thisWeekOutdoors.summary: This week: learn how slope and recent rain shape fruiting. Weekend: walk two aspects of the same hill and write what you find — harvest nothing you cannot identify with confidence.
thisWeekOutdoors.weekday: Study the three lessons below. Sketch one tree species and note which side of the hill it grows on.
thisWeekOutdoors.weekend: Walk a north-facing and south-facing slope for 30 minutes each. Record smell of soil, leaf litter depth, and any fungal signs — photographs OK, collection not required.

speciesSpotlight.title: Yellow morel — last calls in the valleys
speciesSpotlight.summary: The honeycombed cap on a white stem tells you nothing by itself — habitat and season tell the story. In Pike County this week, check ash–elm edges, old apple ground, and river terraces before heat finishes the lowland flush.
fieldMarks: Cap honeycombed; attached to stem at base
Hollow through cap and stem when sliced lengthwise
Often near dying elm, ash, apple, or tulip poplar
Appears when soil warms after rain — not every year the same week
speciesSpotlight.lookAlikes: False morels (Gyromitra) — brain-like, not honeycombed; not hollow. Never eat uncertain fungi.
speciesSpotlight.ethics: Cut at soil line; leave small specimens; never trespass; never share exact harvest spots publicly.

predictionPreview.title: Season table · moisture & fruiting window
predictionPreview.summary: The prediction tool reads weather, slope, and forest type to suggest where to look — not what you will find. It is a field guide for your legs, not a guarantee.
predictionPreview.toolFit: Use the heat map to compare north vs. south slopes and recent rain — then go confirm with your eyes.

legend.label: Unlikely this week
legend.desc: Dry south aspect; wait for next rain

predictionPreview.disclaimer: Placeholder map — real model connects to county elevation, forest cover, and 7-day precipitation.

lessons.title: Morels 101
lessons.subtitle: Observe · Identify · Ethics
lessons.summary: Before taste comes habitat. Learn ash–elm context, the hollow stem test, and why location privacy matters.
lessons.outdoor: Find one dead elm or apple on public land — note aspect and ground cover. Do not pick.

lessons.title: Chanterelles 101
lessons.subtitle: Too early to harvest — not too early to learn
lessons.summary: Golden chanterelles follow oak and moisture in summer. Study gill-like ridges, the peppery smell, and why June rain on Pike County ridges matters.
lessons.outdoor: Locate a mature oak stand on a map. Visit this weekend and note litter depth and moss — no mushrooms required yet.

lessons.title: Forest Moisture 101
lessons.subtitle: Why the prediction tool asks about slope
lessons.summary: North slopes hold moisture; south slopes dry first. Rain history, canopy, and soil texture shape who fruits where.
lessons.outdoor: Dig nothing — press a finger into leaf litter on two aspects. Record which holds moisture longer.

weekendInvestigation.title: Two slopes, one question
weekendInvestigation.drivingQuestion: Does the north-facing slope hold enough moisture to support different fungal signs than the south-facing slope?
weekendInvestigation.when: Saturday morning, 7–10 a.m. — before afternoon storms
weekendInvestigation.place: Any public hill with mixed oak and hemlock — DWGNRA ridges, State Game Lands, or your nearest wooded slope
steps: Pick one hill you can access legally. Mark north and south footpaths.
North slope (20 min): soil smell, litter depth, three plant species, any fungal sign.
South slope (20 min): same protocol. Do not collect mushrooms — photograph if unsure.
ASK: Which slope would you return after a two-day rain? Why?
CONNECT: How would chanterelle timing differ from morel timing here?
REFLECT: What did you assume before you knelt down?
weekendInvestigation.toolPrompt: After your walk, open the season table and compare your notes to the moisture preview — did the map match the ground?

featuredVideo.title: How to read forest moisture before you hunt mushrooms
featuredVideo.summary: Slope, litter, and smell before species names. Ends with a weekend assignment on two aspects of one hill.

fieldNotesPrompt.title: Your field notes — private by default
lines: Three observations — species, sign, or substrate
Optional: sketch of tree bark or leaf — labels later
fieldNotesPrompt.reminder: These notes are yours. They are not a feed. Share only if you choose.

citizenScience.title: Optional contribution
citizenScience.body: You may anonymously note phenology or fruiting phase at county level — never exact harvest locations. Identity not required. Opt out entirely anytime.
